1. Introduction and Project Overview

1.1 The Story We’re Telling

What our project seeks to answer –> what the final visualization will show. This is a concise, one-paragraph summary of the narrative we want to communicate through our visualization. It should be specific about the key insights or messages we aim to convey to our audience. [TO UPDATE]

1.2 Source & Context

The original visualization was published by FYT Consultants in a blog post titled “A Data-Driven Look at Singapore’s Graduate Unemployment Debate” (September 2025), available at:

https://www.fytconsultants.com/single-post/a-data-driven-look-at-singapore-s-graduate-unemployment-debate

The underlying data comes from the Singapore Department of Statistics (SingStat), specifically the annual dataset: Singapore Residents Aged 25 Years and Over by Highest Qualification Attained (HQA), Sex and Age Group.

1.3 The Original Visualization

Figure 1: Original Tableau visualization — "Population by Education and Gender Over Time – 2024"
Source: FYT Consultants / Singapore Department of Statistics

1.3.1 The Story It Tells

The visualization is embedded within a broader article arguing that Singapore may be producing more tertiary graduates than the economy has PMET (Professional, Managerial, Executive & Technical) roles to absorb. The specific chart displays:

  • The proportion (%) of residents in each education level per age group (top stacked bar)
  • The absolute count of male and female residents per age group (bottom bar chart)
  • Data frozen at 2024, with 5 education categories and 9 age groups

Reading left to right, a striking generational divide emerges: residents aged 25–44 are majority university-educated, while those aged 55 and above skew heavily towards below-secondary qualifications. This pattern reflects Singapore’s dramatic educational upgrading over four decades.

2. Critital Analysis of Original Visualization

2.1 Strengths

✅ S1 — Dual encoding of proportion and magnitude
The top chart shows relative composition (what share each education level holds), while the bottom shows absolute counts. This dual-panel approach lets readers answer two different questions simultaneously — "Which education group dominates?" and "How large is each cohort?" — without requiring a mental switch between chart types.
✅ S2 — Intuitive sequential colour palette
The colour gradient from dark navy (University, highest) through teal, pink, green, to olive-yellow (Below Secondary, lowest) aligns naturally with the education hierarchy. Readers do not need to consult the legend repeatedly — colour itself encodes rank.
✅ S3 — Gender disaggregation within each age group
Separating Male and Female bars within every age group enables direct gender comparison, revealing — for example — that females in the 25–44 range actually have higher university attainment rates than males, a non-obvious finding that would be lost in a gender-aggregated view.
✅ S4 — Natural left-to-right generational narrative
Ordering age groups from youngest (left) to oldest (right) creates an intuitive reading direction that maps onto the flow of time. Viewers naturally follow the education "gradient" as a story of national progress.
✅ S5 — Provocative, policy-relevant title
"Too Many Graduates, Too Few PMET Jobs?" frames the data as a question, inviting critical engagement rather than passive reading. It anchors the chart within a live national debate, giving the visualization immediate relevance.

2.2 Limitation and Weaknesses

❌ W1 — No temporal dimension
The chart is a single-year (2024) snapshot. It cannot answer whether educational upgrading is accelerating, plateauing, or reversing. A viewer has no way to know if 58% university attainment in the 25–29 group is a recent surge or a long-standing plateau. The dataset covers 1990–2024 — 34 years of trend data are left completely unused.
❌ W2 — No labour market linkage
The title promises a narrative about PMET job supply vs. graduate supply, but the chart only shows the supply side (educational attainment). There is no employment, labour force, or job vacancy data shown. Readers must accept the "oversupply" conclusion on faith, without any demand-side evidence presented visually.
❌ W3 — Cluttered x-axis with 18 diagonal labels
Each age group generates two bars (Female, Male), resulting in 18 bars with rotated text. The diagonal labels are hard to read and force viewers to tilt their head. A cleaner layout — such as small multiples or a diverging chart — would eliminate this visual noise while conveying the same information.
❌ W4 — Distorted absolute chart due to the 65+ "catch-all" bin
The "65 Years & Over" age group accumulates everyone aged 65 to 90+, resulting in a population of ~781,000 — more than double any other 5-year band. This dwarfs all other bars in the absolute count chart, compressing the visual differences between the working-age groups (25–64) that are most relevant to the PMET jobs story.
❌ W5 — Dual-chart misalignment
The percentage chart (top) and absolute count chart (bottom) share the same x-axis categories but have completely different y-scales and visual logic. There is no visual bridge between them. A reader looking at the top chart cannot intuit population size from it, and vice versa — the connection is implicit at best.
❌ W6 — No annotations or callouts
Key inflection points — such as the age group where university becomes the majority, or the cohort that first crossed 50% — are not highlighted. Readers must scan all bars and mentally compute thresholds. Strategic annotations would dramatically reduce cognitive load and make the key insight immediately visible.
❌ W7 — Only 3 active data dimensions
The chart encodes: Education Level, Age Group, and Gender — only 3 dimensions. The dataset supports at least 6 (adding: Year, Count vs. Percentage, and linkable Labour Force size). Using so few dimensions on such a rich dataset leaves significant analytical depth unexplored.

3. Proposed Improvements

3.1 Enhancement Strategy

Based on the weaknesses identified above, we propose five concrete improvements:

# Weakness Addressed Improvement Method
I1 W1 — No time trend Add a time-series chart (2000–2024) showing university attainment % rising by age cohort Line chart, Chart.js
I2 W2 — No labour market data Add labour force composition by education (2010–2024) to show demand-side context Stacked area/bar chart
I3 W3, W4 — Layout clutter, distortion Replace the dual-panel with a diverging butterfly chart (Females ← → Males), excluding 65+ outlier where appropriate Horizontal diverging bar
I4 W6 — No annotations Add threshold line at 50% and call out the year each cohort crossed majority-graduate status Annotation layer
I5 W7 — Few dimensions Encode 6 dimensions: Age Group, Education, Gender, Year, Count, Labour Force Size Multi-chart dashboard

3.2 Justification for Redesign Approach

Rather than reconstructing the Tableau chart identically, we redesign it as an interactive HTML dashboard (rendered in Quarto). This is justified because:

  1. The original is a static image with no interactivity; tooltips and filters greatly enhance explorability
  2. A Quarto document allows the visualization and its explanation to coexist — fulfilling both the “effective communication” and “aesthetically pleasing and professional” requirements
  3. The enriched dataset (Labour Force + Employment Rate) adds dimensions that cannot be shoe-horned into the original’s layout without a redesign

3.3 Proposed Visualization Design

Interactive Horizontally Stacked Butterfly Chart

Chart Type: Butterfly Chart with interactive filters and tooltip features

Visual Design structure:

The three charts below constitute our improved visualization. They are fully interactive (hover for values) and together encode 6 dimensions: Age Group, Education Level, Gender, Year, Population Count, and Labour Force Size.

Chart 2: Education Breakdown by Gender — Age 30–34 (2024) Improves W3: Cleaner gender layout Improves W4: Working-age focus
Diverging bar chart: females extend left (orange), males extend right (navy). Bars show number of residents. In the 30–34 group, females outpace males in university attainment (65.9% vs 57.7%).

💡 Key insight: Among 30–34 year olds, there are 100,500 female university graduates vs 81,200 male — females have both a higher share (65.9%) and higher absolute count. The original chart's layout made this near-impossible to see clearly.

Interaction Features: Hovering over any bar segment reveals a tooltip with the exact count and percentage for that education level.

Toggle Filters: Dropdowns allow users to switch between age groups (25–29, 30–34, etc.) and years (2000–2024), dynamically updating the chart to show how the gender gap in educational attainment has evolved over time and across cohorts.

3.3.2 Key Design Decisions

  1. Diverging butterfly layout: Clear left-right separation of genders while maintaining the same education categories on the y-axis. This eliminates diagonal labels and allows for direct visual comparison.

  2. Colour consistency: We maintain the original’s education-level colour scheme for continuity, while using a diverging layout to separate genders.

4. Data Sources & Data Quality

selected Data Sources

We use all three datasets provided, each sourced from Singapore Department of Statistics (SingStat) via data.gov.sg:

# Dataset Time Span Format Role in Project
1 Singapore Residents Aged 25+ by HQA, Sex & Age Group (Annual) 1990–2024 CSV (wide) Primary — direct source of the original visualization
2 Resident Labour Force Aged 15+ by HQA and Sex, End June (Annual) 1997–2025 CSV (wide) Enrichment — adds labour market supply dimension
3 Employment Rate of Residents Aged 25–64 (Total) 1991–2022 CSV (long) Context — validates whether the market absorbed graduates

Dataset Justifications

Dataset 1 directly matches the original visualization and contains 35 years of history that were ignored in the original chart (Weakness W1). Dataset 2 allows us to close the “PMET jobs” narrative gap (Weakness W2) by showing how the composition of the labour force itself has shifted. Dataset 3 provides a macro check: if employment rates stayed high despite graduate surges, the “oversupply crisis” framing may be overstated.

Together, the three datasets yield 6 active dimensions in our improved visualization: Age Group, Education Level, Gender, Year (2000–2024), Population Count, and Labour Force Count — well exceeding the 4-dimension minimum requirement.

4.3 Data Quality Assessment

4.3.1 Strengths

  1. Trusted Sources: All three datasets are official government statistics, collected through rigorous surveys and administrative records. They are widely cited in academic research and policy discussions, lending them high credibility. The data is complete for the specified time spans, with no known major revisions or anomalies.

  2. Standardized Definitions: The datasets use consistent definitions for key variables (e.g., education levels, age groups) across years, enabling valid longitudinal analysis. The “Highest Qualification Attained” categories are well-defined and align with international standards.

4.3.2 Limitations

  1. Inconsistent Formatting: The raw CSV files are in wide format with years as column headers, and hierarchical data encoded through indentation in the “DataSeries” column. This non-tidy structure requires significant cleaning and transformation to be usable for visualization.

  2. Missing Data: The Labour Force dataset uses “na” string literals for missing years (pre-2008), which must be handled carefully to avoid misinterpretation as zero values.

  3. Age Group Binning: The “65 Years & Over” category aggregates a wide range of ages, distorting comparisons with the more granular 5-year bands. This requires careful consideration in the visualization to avoid misleading impressions about the working-age population.

5. Data Engineering Workflow

5.1 Raw Data Structure

All three SingStat files arrive in wide format: years as column headers, with a hierarchical DataSeries column that encodes multiple attributes (gender × education × age group) through indentation and naming conventions.

Example of raw structure (Dataset 1):

DataSeries                              | 2024   | 2023   | ...
Total                                   | 3145000| 3100900| ...
    25 - 29 Years                       | 222000 | 238000 | ...
    30 - 34 Years                       | 293200 | 307600 | ...
...
Total Males - University                | ...    | ...    | ...
    25 - 29 Years                       | 55800  | 60200  | ...

Key structural challenges: - Age groups appear as child rows beneath their parent (gender × education) row, identified only by leading whitespace and positional index - “Secondary” is labelled inconsistently as "Total- Secondary" (no space before dash) vs. "Total - Below Secondary" (space before dash) - Dataset 2 uses "na" string literals for missing years (pre-2008) rather than blank cells

5.2 Cleaning Decisions

Issue Decision Rationale
Hierarchical rows encoded via indentation Strip whitespace, then extract child rows by positional offset (next 9 rows after each parent) Structure is perfectly consistent throughout file; regex would be brittle
"Total- Secondary" (no space) Hard-coded exception in key lookup Single known anomaly; explicit is safer than a fuzzy match
"na" strings in Labour Force data pd.to_numeric(errors='coerce') → NaN; excluded from chart for those years Avoids silent zero-imputation for genuinely missing historical data
Wide format (years as columns) pd.melt() to long format Required by all chart libraries; enables filtering by year
65+ group distorts absolute counts Retained in % chart; flagged in absolute chart with visual note Suppressing data would misrepresent the population; annotation is cleaner
Different year ranges across datasets Left-join on Year using Dataset 1 as primary; gaps shown as missing in chart Dataset 1 is the primary source; others are supplementary

5.3 Annotated Pipeline Code

# ============================================================
# Data Engineering Pipeline
# Singapore Education & Workforce Project
# ============================================================

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# ── 1. LOAD ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
pop_raw = pd.read_csv("SingaporeResidents_HQA_Sex_AgeGroup.csv")
lf_raw  = pd.read_csv("ResidentLabourForce_HQA_Sex.csv")
emp_raw = pd.read_csv("EmploymentRate_25to64.csv")

# ── 2. CLEAN LABELS ──────────────────────────────────────────
# Leading/trailing whitespace in DataSeries is structural in the raw file
pop_raw["DataSeries"] = pop_raw["DataSeries"].str.strip()
lf_raw["DataSeries"]  = lf_raw["DataSeries"].str.strip()

# ── 3. DEFINE CONSTANTS ──────────────────────────────────────
AGE_GROUPS = [
    "25 - 29 Years", "30 - 34 Years", "35 - 39 Years",
    "40 - 44 Years", "45 - 49 Years", "50 - 54 Years",
    "55 - 59 Years", "60 - 64 Years", "65 Years & Over"
]
EDU_LEVELS = [
    "Below Secondary", "Secondary",
    "Post Secondary (Non-Tertiary)",
    "Diploma & Professional Qualification", "University"
]
YEARS = [str(y) for y in range(2000, 2025)]

# ── 4. EXTRACT HELPER FUNCTION ───────────────────────────────
def extract_block(df, key, age_groups, years):
    """
    Locate a parent DataSeries row by exact key match,
    then extract the 9 age-group child rows below it.
    Returns long-format DataFrame: (age_group, year, count).
    """
    idx = df[df["DataSeries"] == key].index[0]
    records = []
    for ag in age_groups:
        sub = df.iloc[idx + 1 : idx + 10]
        row = sub[sub["DataSeries"] == ag]
        if row.empty:
            continue
        for yr in years:
            try:
                val = int(row[yr].values[0])
            except (ValueError, IndexError):
                val = np.nan
            records.append({"age_group": ag, "year": int(yr), "count": val})
    return pd.DataFrame(records)

# ── 5. BUILD TIDY POPULATION TABLE ───────────────────────────
GENDER_MAP = {
    "Total":         "Both",
    "Total Males":   "Males",
    "Total Females": "Females"
}

all_records = []
for gender_key, gender_label in GENDER_MAP.items():

    # Denominator: total population for this gender × age × year
    total_block = extract_block(
        pop_raw, gender_key, AGE_GROUPS, YEARS
    ).rename(columns={"count": "total"})

    for edu in EDU_LEVELS:
        # Quirk: "Secondary" has inconsistent dash spacing
        if gender_key == "Total":
            edu_key = ("Total- Secondary"
                       if edu == "Secondary"
                       else f"Total - {edu}")
        else:
            edu_key = f"{gender_key} - {edu}"

        block = extract_block(pop_raw, edu_key, AGE_GROUPS, YEARS)
        if block.empty:
            continue

        merged = block.merge(total_block, on=["age_group", "year"])
        merged["pct"]       = (merged["count"] / merged["total"] * 100).round(2)
        merged["gender"]    = gender_label
        merged["education"] = edu
        all_records.append(merged)

pop_tidy = pd.concat(all_records, ignore_index=True)
# Shape: ~3,375 rows × 7 cols (age_group, year, count, total, pct, gender, education)

# ── 6. CLEAN LABOUR FORCE DATA ───────────────────────────────
yr_cols = sorted(c for c in lf_raw.columns if c.isdigit())
lf_long = lf_raw.melt(
    id_vars=["DataSeries"], value_vars=yr_cols,
    var_name="year", value_name="lf_thousands"
)
lf_long["year"]         = lf_long["year"].astype(int)
lf_long["lf_thousands"] = pd.to_numeric(
    lf_long["lf_thousands"], errors="coerce"  # "na" → NaN
)

# ── 7. EXPORT VISUALIZATION-READY FILES ──────────────────────
# Snapshot: 2024 cross-section for redesigned bar/butterfly chart
pop_tidy[pop_tidy["year"] == 2024].to_csv("snapshot_2024.csv", index=False)

# Time series: 2000–2024, both genders, for trend chart
pop_tidy[pop_tidy["gender"] == "Both"].to_csv("timeseries.csv", index=False)

# Labour force: 2010–2024 by education category
lf_long[lf_long["year"] >= 2010].to_csv("labourforce.csv", index=False)

print("✅ Pipeline complete.")

6. Insights & Analysis

Using the cleaned data and improved visualizations, we derive three key analytical insights:

Insight 1 — The graduate majority tipping point.
Among residents aged 25–34, university graduates became the majority around 2013–2015 (Chart 1). This is a cohort-level phenomenon: those born from the late 1980s onward entered a university system that had dramatically expanded capacity. By 2024, over 60% of residents in the 30–44 age bands hold degrees — the highest in Singapore’s history.

Insight 2 — Females are outpacing males in educational attainment.
In the 30–34 age group, female university graduates number 100,500 vs 81,200 for males — a gap of nearly 20,000 (Chart 2). Female university attainment (65.9%) exceeds male (57.7%) by 8 percentage points. This gender reversal in educational outcomes is invisible in the original visualization’s cluttered layout but has significant implications for career pathway planning and workforce policy.

Insight 3 — Labour force degree holders nearly doubled in 14 years.
Degree holders in the resident labour force grew from 565,000 (2010) to 1,061,000 (2024) — an 88% increase (Chart 3). Over the same period, below-secondary workers fell from 455,000 to 280,000 (−39%). The labour force is structurally shifting toward high-qualification workers, which supports the PMET-demand argument — but also raises valid concerns about whether job creation has matched this pace.

Planned Analysis Methods: - Year-on-year growth rate of university share by cohort (pct_change()) - Cohort-tracking: following the 25–29 group of 2000 as they move into older age bands over successive years - Gender parity index: female-to-male university attainment ratio by age group and year - Labour-market absorption rate: ratio of degree-holder population growth to labour force degree-holder growth

7. Planned Work Distribution

Team Member Primary Tasks Est. Hours
Member A Data acquisition, cleaning pipeline (Steps 1–4), reproducibility check ~12 hrs
Member B Feature engineering, dataset integration, cohort-tracking analysis ~12 hrs
Member C Chart 1 (time-series) + Chart 3 (labour force), Quarto setup & theming ~12 hrs
Member D Chart 2 (butterfly redesign), critique write-up, annotation layer ~12 hrs
All Members Revealjs slides, MP4 recording, scripts, peer review ~8 hrs each

Note: Distribution will be adjusted based on implementation complexity. All members are responsible for reviewing one another’s code, visualizations, and written sections before final submission.
